Arguments.prompt: Download US state boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip and US county boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip. Unzip both archives. Load the state shapefile (cb_2018_us_state_20m.shp) using geopandas and filter to the single row where the NAME column equals 'Oregon'. Load the county shapefile (cb_2018_us_county_5m.shp) using geopandas and filter to the single row where the NAME column equals 'Benton' and the STATEFP column matches Oregon's STATEFP value. Reproject both GeoDataFrames to EPSG:4326 if they are not already in that CRS. Compute the bounding box of Benton County by calling the total_bounds property on the county GeoDataFrame, which returns (minx, miny, maxx, maxy) in longitude/latitude. Expand this bounding box by 0.5 degrees in each direction: outer_minx = minx - 0.5, outer_miny = miny - 0.5, outer_maxx = maxx + 0.5, outer_maxy = maxy + 0.5. Create a GeoDataFrame for the outer boundary as a single rectangular polygon using shapely.geometry.box(outer_minx, outer_miny, outer_maxx, outer_maxy) with CRS EPSG:4326. Download the NOAA GHCND station inventory from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/pub/data/ghcn/daily/ghcnd-inventory.txt. This file is a fixed-width text file with columns: STATION (characters 1-11), LATITUDE (characters 13-20), LONGITUDE (characters 22-30), ELEMENT (characters 32-35), FIRSTYEAR (characters 37-40), LASTYEAR (characters 42-45). Parse it using pandas read_fwf with colspecs=[(0,11),(12,20),(21,30),(31,35),(36,40),(41,45)] and names=['STATION','LATITUDE','LONGITUDE','ELEMENT','FIRSTYEAR','LASTYEAR']. Keep only rows where ELEMENT is 'TMAX' or 'TMIN' and where LASTYEAR is greater than or equal to the current calendar year. For each station that appears in this filtered set, keep it only if it has BOTH a TMAX row and a TMIN row. Then filter those stations to only those where LATITUDE is between outer_miny and outer_maxy and LONGITUDE is between outer_minx and outer_maxx. Deduplicate so each STATION ID appears once, retaining its LATITUDE and LONGITUDE. Create a GeoDataFrame from these stations using shapely.geometry.Point(longitude, latitude) for each row, with CRS EPSG:4326. Save the Oregon state geometry as State.geojson, the Benton County geometry as County.geojson, the outer boundary rectangle as OuterBoundary.geojson, and the filtered stations as Stations.geojson. Upload all four files to the S3 data store under the folder WeatherGeographicPlot: paths are WeatherGeographicPlot/State.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/County.geojson, WeatherGeographicPlot/OuterBoundary.geojson, and WeatherGeographicPlot/Stations.geojson.

Download the file WeatherGeographicPlot/Stations.geojson from the S3 data store and load it as a GeoDataFrame using geopandas. Each feature has a STATION attribute (the GHCND station ID string) and a geometry point. Determine the target week as follows: take today's date (using datetime.date.today()), subtract 28 days to get a reference date, then find the most recent Monday on or before that reference date by subtracting (reference_date.weekday()) days — this is the week_start. The week_end is week_start plus 6 days (the following Sunday). For each station in the GeoDataFrame, construct the download URL as https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/global-historical-climatology-network-daily/access/<STATION_ID>.csv where <STATION_ID> is the STATION attribute value. Download the CSV using requests or urllib. The CSV has a header row; relevant columns are DATE (format YYYY-MM-DD), TMIN, and TMAX. Load the CSV into a pandas DataFrame. Parse the DATE column as datetime. Filter rows to only those where DATE is between week_start and week_end inclusive. Drop any rows where TMIN or TMAX is missing (NaN). If after filtering and dropping there are no rows remaining for a station, skip that station entirely. For stations with valid data, compute the mean of TMIN across all remaining rows and the mean of TMAX across all remaining rows. Divide both means by 10.0 to convert from tenths of a degree Celsius to whole degrees Celsius. Collect results into a list of records with keys STATION, TMIN (averaged, converted), TMAX (averaged, converted), and geometry (the Point from the original GeoDataFrame). Build a GeoDataFrame from these records with CRS EPSG:4326 and columns STATION, geometry, TMIN, TMAX. Save this GeoDataFrame as TemperatureData.geojson and upload it to the S3 data store at path WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureData.geojson.

Download the following four files from the S3 data store folder WeatherGeographicPlot: OuterBoundary.geojson, TemperatureData.geojson, State.geojson, and County.geojson. Load each as a GeoDataFrame using geopandas. From OuterBoundary.geojson, extract the bounding box using total_bounds to get (outer_minx, outer_miny, outer_maxx, outer_maxy). From TemperatureData.geojson, extract station longitudes as [geom.x for geom in gdf.geometry], latitudes as [geom.y for geom in gdf.geometry], TMIN values, and TMAX values. Determine the target week for the figure title: take today's date (datetime.date.today()), subtract 28 days, then back up to the most recent Monday by subtracting (reference_date.weekday()) days to get week_start; week_end is week_start plus 6 days. Build a 100x100 interpolation grid: grid_lon = numpy.linspace(outer_minx, outer_maxx, 100) and grid_lat = numpy.linspace(outer_miny, outer_maxy, 100), then grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d = numpy.meshgrid(grid_lon, grid_lat). Use scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='cubic' to interpolate TMIN values onto the grid: grid_tmin = scipy.interpolate.griddata((lons, lats), tmin_values, (grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d), method='cubic'). Do the same for TMAX: grid_tmax = scipy.interpolate.griddata((lons, lats), tmax_values, (grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d), method='cubic'). Create a matplotlib figure with two side-by-side subplots using fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 7)). On ax1 (TMIN): call ax1.contourf(grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d, grid_tmin, levels=20, cmap='Blues_r', alpha=0.8). Add a colorbar to ax1 labeled 'Temperature (°C)'. Scatter the station points on ax1 using ax1.scatter(lons, lats, c=tmin_values, cmap='Blues_r', edgecolors='black', zorder=5). Plot the State.geojson boundary on ax1 using state_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax1, color='black', linewidth=1). Plot the County.geojson boundary on ax1 using county_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax1, color='darkgray', linewidth=1.5). Set ax1 xlim to (outer_minx, outer_maxx) and ylim to (outer_miny, outer_maxy). Set ax1 xlabel to 'Longitude' and ylabel to 'Latitude'. Set ax1 xticks using numpy.arange(round(outer_minx, 1), outer_maxx, 0.5) and yticks using numpy.arange(round(outer_miny, 1), outer_maxy, 0.5). Set ax1 title to 'Minimum Temperature Heatmap (°C)'. Set ax1 aspect to 'equal'. On ax2 (TMAX): call ax2.contourf(grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d, grid_tmax, levels=20, cmap='Reds', alpha=0.8). Add a colorbar to ax2 labeled 'Temperature (°C)'. Scatter the station points on ax2 using ax2.scatter(lons, lats, c=tmax_values, cmap='Reds', edgecolors='black', zorder=5). Plot the State.geojson boundary on ax2 using state_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax2, color='black', linewidth=1). Plot the County.geojson boundary on ax2 using county_gdf.boundary.plot(ax=ax2, color='darkgray', linewidth=1.5). Set ax2 xlim to (outer_minx, outer_maxx) and ylim to (outer_miny, outer_maxy). Set ax2 xlabel to 'Longitude' and ylabel to 'Latitude'. Set ax2 xticks using numpy.arange(round(outer_minx, 1), outer_maxx, 0.5) and yticks using numpy.arange(round(outer_miny, 1), outer_maxy, 0.5). Set ax2 title to 'Maximum Temperature Heatmap (°C)'. Set ax2 aspect to 'equal'. Format week_start as a string like 'Monday, January 6, 2025' using week_start.strftime('%A, %B %-d, %Y') and week_end similarly. Set the figure supertitle using fig.suptitle(f'Temperature Heatmap for Benton County for week starting {week_start_str} through {week_end_str}', fontsize=14). Call plt.tight_layout(). Save the figure as TemperatureHeatmap.png using plt.savefig('TemperatureHeatmap.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight'). Upload TemperatureHeatmap.png to the S3 data store at path WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureHeatmap.png. Also upload OuterBoundary.geojson, TemperatureData.geojson, State.geojson, and County.geojson back to WeatherGeographicPlot/ if any local modifications were made, ensuring all intermediate files are present in the data store under WeatherGeographicPlot/.
